claims.text: In his 1998 book Penetration, Ingo Swan claimed the CIA employed him to remote view the dark side of the Moon to look for alien presence.
entities: location:the dark side of the moon

evidence.quote:
when Ingo Swan released his book titled
penetration in 1998 he claimed to be a
psychic who was employed by the CIA to
re

claims.rationale: The existence of Swan's 1998 book 'Penetration' and its lunar-alien claims are matters of published record, verifiable from the book itself. The book making this claim is true; whether the CIA actually tasked him with lunar remote viewing is a separate question addressed by later claims.

claims.text: Declassified CIA documents released starting in 2006 confirm Ingo Swan's participation in the Stargate project remote-viewing program.

evidence.quote:
ounded like science fiction but in
2006 when the CIA started releasing
documents on the Stargate project Swan's
participation in the program was
confirmed when

claims.rationale: The CIA's Stargate Project declassification and the documents naming Swan are publicly archived at the CIA FOIA reading room and widely verified. Host confirms this late in the video ('if you search the CIA website there are hundreds of documents').

claims.text: Unfriendly extraterrestrials are already on Earth and are building something on the far side of the Moon.
entities: location:the far side of the moon

evidence.quote:
ly were they already here but they
are building something on The Far Side
of the Moon and according to Swan these
aliens aren't fr

claims.rationale: Swan's central thesis. No independent corroboration exists: no space-agency imagery, no recovered artifact, no remote-sensing anomaly. The claim rests entirely on Swan's alleged remote-viewing, which is not accepted by mainstream physics. Host presents Swan as sincere but does not endorse the conclusion.

claims.text: In a 1973 Stanford Research Institute experiment, Ingo Swan was asked to remote view Jupiter before NASA's Pioneer probes arrived.

evidence.quote:
system
there was an experiment in 1973 where
Ingo Swan was asked to project his
Consciousness to the planet Jupiter at
first Swan rejected the idea Ino Swan
ne

claims.rationale: The 1973 Swan/Harold Puthoff Jupiter remote-viewing session at SRI is documented in multiple parapsychology papers and Swan's own writings. That the experiment was conducted and yielded specific written observations is well-attested; the accuracy of those observations is addressed in later claims.

claims.text: Swan predicted Jupiter has a ring made of dust and tiny asteroids, which Voyager 1 confirmed in 1979.

evidence.quote:
en Voyager One passed
by Jupiter in 1979 tell me there was a
ring there was a ring
[Music]
YY I am not this body in terms of
Consciousness and Consciousness can go
p

claims.rationale: Voyager 1 did discover Jupiter's rings in 1979 — that part is established fact. The claim that Swan predicted this in 1973 rests on his own written notes; skeptics note such notes were not independently sealed/witnessed in a verifiable protocol, so the 'prediction' claim is weaker than the 'Voyager found rings' fact.

claims.text: Skeptics argue Swan's Jupiter observations are trivially deducible from astronomical knowledge already available in 1973.

evidence.quote:
Skeptics will say all these observations
could be deduced like of course Jupiter
has violent storms we can see them same
with the color of clouds infrared and
Atmos

claims.rationale: The skeptical rebuttal that most of Swan's observations (storms, hydrogen, cloud color) were either already known or trivially inferrable is widely made by mainstream scientists. Host raises this specifically to address it before pivoting to the ring as the 'new' item.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] If observations were deducible from priors, the 'prediction' has less probative weight — but the ring claim partially escapes this critique.

claims.text: In March 1975 Ingo Swan was covertly recruited in Washington DC by a man calling himself Mr Axelrod for a classified remote-viewing project.

evidence.quote:
in March 1975 ingos
Swan received a call at 3:00 in the
morning he was told to go to Washington
and visit the Museum of Natural History

claims.rationale: The story's sole source is Swan's own 1998 book. The specifics (museum rendezvous, hooded car, twins, cigar-smoking Axelrod) are theatrical and uncorroborated. No declassified document names Axelrod, and the CIA's declassified Stargate materials do not include this side mission. Plausible as embellished memoir, low as literal history.

claims.text: Axelrod offered Swan $11,000 per day (over $60,000 in 2020s dollars) to participate in a secret remote-viewing project with strict non-disclosure terms.

evidence.quote:
an to participate in a remote
viewing project for his effort he would
be paid $11,000 a day in cash which in
today's money is over $6,000 a day inl
huh y are we

claims.rationale: Rests on same single-source (Swan's book) as c_0007. $11,000/day cash payments in the mid-1970s would leave financial traces; none produced. Host notes the 'today's money $6,000' figure mid-sentence which is actually an error (should be much larger — inflation from 1975 is ~6x), suggesting uncritical rendering of Swan's text.

dependencies.rationale: The offer only exists if the Axelrod recruitment story is true.

claims.text: Remote viewing the Moon, Swan saw tractor tracks, wind-patterned dunes, and diffused green light in a crater — all impossible on the airless lunar surface.

evidence.quote:
escribes
seeing tracks on the surface tracks that
seem to have been made by tractors or
some kind of Machinery he sees patterns
that look like they were made by wind

claims.rationale: Physical implausibility: the Moon's vacuum makes wind-patterned dunes impossible and diffused light requires an atmosphere. Swan's 'impossible' observation is internally consistent with either a genuine alien atmosphere dome OR a confabulation; extraordinary-claim standards put directTruth very low without independent evidence.

claims.text: Swan observed humanoid figures inside domed structures on the Moon, who noticed his projected consciousness and pointed at him.

evidence.quote:
s inside he describes
humanoid figures that look almost like
us he describes this as an area of high
activity whoever or whatever these
beings are they're very busy and S

claims.rationale: Climactic story beat with zero corroborating evidence. The 'they see me' moment is dramatically convenient and requires accepting both the metaphysical reality of remote-viewing and the existence of psychically-sensitive lunar humanoids. No physical artifacts, imagery, or testimony from other viewers confirm this specific scene.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the Moon-with-atmosphere scene with the climactic humanoid encounter.
Provides the specific episode that underwrites Swan's thesis that aliens are building on the Moon.

claims.text: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin reportedly told NASA's chief medical officer from the lunar surface that aliens were parked on the crater watching them.

evidence.quote:
uzz were on the lunar
surface Neil switched to the the medical
Channel and spoke directly with the
chief medical officer saying they're
here they're parked on the side of

claims.rationale: This is a long-debunked hoax transcript often attributed to a Soviet ham-radio intercept; no authentic NASA audio or Armstrong/Aldrin statement corroborates it. Both astronauts denied such exchanges. Host inserts it as evocative filler without sourcing.

dependencies.rationale: Narratively paired with the Swan lunar-humanoid claim as independent corroboration.

claims.text: The beautiful supermarket woman Swan believed was an alien was more plausibly a Soviet spy surveilling him during the Cold War psychic arms race.

evidence.quote:
s that she was
a Soviet spy honey yeah I I think so
this was the 1970s and during this time
the United States and Soviet Union were
in the middle of what you mi

claims.rationale: Host's preferred naturalistic alternative. The Cold War US-Soviet psychic-research rivalry is historically documented (KGB's 21st Laboratory, etc.), making a Soviet tail plausible but entirely speculative in this specific case — no evidence identifies the woman. Host presents it as 'the most logical explanation' but with no investigative followup.

dependencies.rationale: Only coherent if the Axelrod recruitment and subsequent surveillance context are accepted.

claims.text: The Alaska UFO encounter Swan witnessed was an alien resupply mission mining water from the lake for a lunar base.

evidence.quote:
ked Swan to continue Swan
said it's a resupply mission they're
mining the moon and they need our water
Axel Rod smiled at this he didn't agree
but he didn't dis

claims.rationale: Swan's own interpretation of the Alaska event. Rests on accepting the encounter itself (single source), accepting the triangle craft was alien rather than classified US tech, and accepting the lunar-mining narrative. Host flags narrative convenience ('very tidy story... he knew exactly where it would appear').

dependencies.rationale: Supplies the supply-chain mechanism for the alien-lunar-base thesis.

claims.text: The Alaska UFO story is suspiciously tidy, and the reported tree-destroying and animal-killing light rays were never independently confirmed.

evidence.quote:
was another tough one for
me it was just a very tidy story Mr Axel
Rod knew exactly where it would appear
how it operated what it would be doing I
mean if he w

claims.rationale: Host explicitly voices skepticism of the Alaska story's narrative convenience and notes the absence of followup on physically-verifiable claims (dead trees, killed animals). This is a rare moment of the host himself debunking part of the source material.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] If the UFO story itself is too-neat and unchecked, the resupply-mission interpretation loses its observational anchor.

claims.text: The US government won't allow independent confirmation of lunar anomalies, withholding far-side Moon photography from the public.

evidence.quote:
US
Government simply will not allow us to
confirm the details there are reports of
astronauts hearing signals coming from
the moon but those signals are dismisse

claims.rationale: Cover-up framing. In fact NASA's LRO has released gigabytes of high-resolution far-side imagery publicly; the 'won't let us confirm' claim is factually wrong. 'Astronauts hearing signals' refers to the well-documented Apollo 10 whistling audio, which has a mundane explanation (VHF interference between LM and CM).
